weather: Cloudy and cool today in Edmonds. High only 57-62 °F, low tonight 41-46 °F. Light south wind 5-10 mph shifting northwest. Rain chance stays low—10-20 %—so most of the day stays dry, just overcast. Evening turns partly cloudy, no showers expected. Good day for a light jacket and the stroller walk to the park.

local_news.findings: Edmonds will begin its 2025 sewer and storm-pipe rehabilitation project on May 4. The Combined Cured-in-Place Pipe (CIPP) work will repair or replace failing sewer and storm lines at 27 locations identified in a 2013 review. Construction is expected to last through mid-summer, with traffic impacts on 6th Ave S, 8th Ave S and 220th St SW. The $419,000 contract was approved unanimously by the City Council on April 7.

The Edmonds City Council unanimously approved a $415,116 contract with Jacobs Engineering to commission the new gasification system at the wastewater treatment plant. Public Works Director Andy Rheaume said the project is at a “critical point” and Jacobs will remain on-site through mid-April to train city staff. The funds come entirely from the wastewater utility and were part of a larger consent-agenda package approved April 7.

South County Fire commissioners are weighing two competing redistricting plans to fold Edmonds’ 42,000-plus residents into the five-district Regional Fire Authority. Plan 1 keeps almost all of Edmonds in a single district; Plan 2 splits the city at Pine Street so that no sitting commissioner is forced to run against another. A public hearing will be scheduled before the board adopts a final map later this year.

Edmonds police arrested an adult female for theft in the 21900 block of Highway 99 and booked her into Lynnwood Jail. In a separate incident, officers arrested an adult male on an outstanding warrant. Both events were logged in the weekly blotter ending April 19.

career.notes: Most districts maintain rolling pools and post specific openings through Frontline/AppliTrack or Red Rover. Check each district’s HR page weekly for new postings; many 2026-27 contracts will be finalized by late May. Bellevue Christian and King’s Schools require faith statements; public districts require valid WA State certification with appropriate endorsements.

family.choir: Seattle Choral Company: Auditions for the 2026-2027 season are scheduled for May 4-6, 2026. The process includes singing two short choral pieces unaccompanied, a vocal warm-up to demonstrate range, interval/pitch testing, and reading passages in Latin, French, German, Italian, and Spanish. Rehearsals are held at Phinney Ridge Lutheran Church. Membership dues are $225 for the full season or $30/month for partial participation. Seattle Pro Musica: Auditions occur each August for the following season. The 2026-27 season auditions have not yet been announced - check their website for updates. Choral Arts Northwest: No current auditions, but they hosted a Music Educator Sing-in on March 15, 2026 at First United Methodist Church.

Imagine Children's Museum: Offers Little Science Lab programs at Lynnwood Library every Monday at 2pm for curious preschoolers and caregivers. Woodland Park Zoo: Zoomazium offers daily indoor play for ages birth-8 from 9:30am-3:00pm, with special toddler programs at 10:30am Saturdays and Sundays. New Forest Trailhead exhibit opens May 1, 2026.

global_news.findings: Israel's complete blockade of humanitarian aid to Gaza since March 2 is being challenged at the International Court of Justice, with the UN accusing Israel of violating international law by refusing to allow aid for 2.3 million residents facing starvation. The hearings mark a critical escalation in the legal battle over Gaza's humanitarian crisis.

Germany commits to dramatically increasing its NATO military role, with President Frank-Walter Steinmeier announcing plans for 500 billion euros in defense spending and positioning Germany as the backbone of European conventional defense amid growing uncertainty about US reliability under Trump.

The UK proposes a new strategic partnership with the EU emphasizing shared values including support for Ukraine's territorial integrity, commitment to the Paris Climate Agreement, and free trade - a direct counter to Trump administration policies that have recognized Russian territorial gains and undermined climate commitments.

The UK government announces it will deny refugee protections to foreign sex offenders, amending the Border Security, Asylum and Immigration Bill to exclude anyone with sex offender register convictions from asylum status as part of broader immigration crackdown measures.

The US-Israeli war on Iran continues to dominate global geopolitics, with Iran's closure of the Strait of Hormuz causing the largest oil supply disruption since the 1970s energy crisis, pushing oil above $100/barrel and forcing emergency strategic reserve releases. The IMF has cut global growth forecasts and expects higher inflation due to the conflict's economic impact.

Russia-Ukraine peace negotiations continue through US-brokered talks, but face major obstacles as Russia demands territorial concessions and opposes Western peacekeeping forces. Recent reports indicate ongoing drone attacks and strikes on both sides, with both Ukraine and Russia accusing each other of hundreds of ceasefire violations.

An assassination attempt on Donald Trump at the White House Correspondents' Dinner has led to charges against the suspect for attempting to kill the president, prompting a security review and fueling various conspiracy theories amid heightened political tensions.

intellectual_journals.source: The New York Review of Books
intellectual_journals.findings: Pankaj Mishra's 'A Bitter Education' (March 13, 2026) delivers a searing meditation on the death of Indo-Iranian civilization under Western supremacism. Mishra traces how Persian language and culture once formed a 1,000-year lingua franca across Asia, from Marco Polo to Tagore's 1932 visit to Iran. The essay pivots to contemporary horror: the US torpedoing of the IRIS Dena, killing nearly 100 unarmed Iranian sailors returning from an Indian naval exercise, met with silence from India's Hindu nationalist government. Mishra diagnoses 'gharbzadegi' (West-struckness) as the spiritual sickness affl [TRUNCATED]

intellectual_journals.source: The New York Review of Books
intellectual_journals.findings: Rosa Lyster's 'This Bitter Earth' (May 14, 2026) reviews Caroline Tracey's 'Salt Lakes: An Unnatural History,' revealing these 'terminal lakes' as climate crisis canaries. Salt lakes—like Utah's Great Salt Lake (70% water loss) and California's Owens Lake (nearly gone)—are disappearing due to irrigation diversion and climate change. The exposed lake beds create toxic dust storms containing arsenic and mercury; women near the vanished Aral Sea are warned against breastfeeding. These ecosystems support 90% of the world's eared grebes and 300 bird species along the Pacific Flyway. Lyster critique [TRUNCATED]

intellectual_journals.source: The New York Review of Books
intellectual_journals.findings: Adam Hanieh's 'A Widening Gulf' (April 11, 2026) exposes the UAE's role as a global petrochemical empire beyond oil stereotypes. The federation—Abu Dhabi's energy dominance plus Dubai's financial hub—controls crucial supply chains: oil-based plastics, natural gas fertilizers, and Jebel Ali (world's largest man-made harbor). Hanieh details how the UAE's authoritarian capitalism relies on a majority migrant workforce without rights, while expanding military interventions in Yemen, Sudan, and Libya. The Iran war has hardened Gulf alignment with US power, though Oman maintains distinct positioning [TRUNCATED]

The April 20, 2026 issue features David Remnick's 'Trump's Strategic and Moral Failure in Iran' in the Comment section, analyzing how Trump's Iran obsession poses emergencies to both America and the world. The issue includes Thomas McGuane's fiction 'Ordinary Wear and Tear' about two men launching a boat, alongside cultural coverage of Isa Genzken's chaos-order art, Raye's ambitious album, and Nathan Lane's Willy Loman. Notable pieces include 'The Return of Family Detention' on Trump's immigrant child detention policies, and 'A Lesson of Vietnam' reviewing books on unwinnable conflicts. The ma [TRUNCATED]

Jon Lee Anderson's 'Is Cuba Next?' (March 30, 2026) chronicles Cuba's collapse through vivid reporting in Havana. With gasoline scarce, residents use horse-drawn carts; frequent blackouts leave only generator-owners with power. A massive exodus and tourism drop have left Havana 'all but abandoned.' Anderson profiles Heidy Sánchez, deported after five years in Florida, now calling her US-citizen husband and daughter twice daily when electricity works. The piece captures Cuba's post-Castro vacuum—Fidel's revolutionary mythos replaced by crisis without unifying leadership. US abduction of Venezue [TRUNCATED]

triadic_ontology.findings: The Triadic Closure of Existence: Co-Emergence of Binding, Transformation, and Relational Readout (15 Apr 2026, Zenodo) argues that reality does not compile linearly but emerges through a simultaneous, phase-locked triadic closure whose three load-bearing roles—stable matter, temporal transformation, and readable relational coupling—are structurally identical to the fundamental physical interactions. Mathematical Foundations of Triadic Closure and Recursive Computational Ontologies (15 Apr 2026, Zenodo) critiques the “Crisis of Distinction” between General Relativity and Quantum Mechanics and proposes the Triadic Closure or “First Braid” as a recursive computational ontology that resolves the impasse. Christopher Isabelle’s Triadic Relational Ontology (PhilArchive) extends his earlier Primordial Separation Ontology, insisting that dyadic structures are insufficient for emergence and meaning; a third, actively maintained relational field is required. Turning to theological physics, Path [TRUNCATED]

ai_systems.findings: The autonomous-research stack is maturing fast. Google Cloud’s new PaperOrchestra (April 2026) shows the clearest end-to-end pipeline yet: five specialized agents turn raw lab notes into submission-ready LaTeX, making ~6 000 LLM calls per paper to auto-generate literature reviews, figures and API-verified citations. A Cambridge-led astro team just open-sourced Cmbagent1, the engine behind the winning entry in the FAIR Universe weak-lensing challenge; the hybrid workflow lets the agents explore autonomously but accepts human nudges when needed, beating expert-only pipelines. Meanwhile, the underlying frameworks are standardizing: the Auton paper (arXiv 2602.23720) formalizes agent execution as an augmented POMDP with hierarchical memory and policy-projection safety, while the Model Context Protocol (MCP) is emerging as the USB-C port for plug-and-play tools. On the prompting side, 2026’s frontier reasoning models—Claude 4.6, GPT-5.4 and Gemini 2.5 Deep Think—have flipped the script: “le [TRUNCATED]

uap.findings: Congress is accelerating UAP disclosure through multiple fronts. Rep. Anna Paulina Luna's House Oversight Task Force demanded 46 specific UAP videos from the Pentagon by April 14, warning of "very real threats" near military installations. After the deadline passed without compliance, Luna is considering subpoena power. President Trump announced "very interesting documents" will be released "very, very soon" following his February directive to declassify UFO files. The Pentagon's AARO office confirmed coordination with the White House for "never-before-seen" releases. Meanwhile, the mysterious death of intelligence officer Matthew Sullivan—scheduled to testify about crash retrieval programs and non-human technology—has intensified cover-up fears, with Rep. Eric Burlison formally requesting FBI investigation into potential witness safety issues.

newyorker.title: The New Yorker
newyorker.dek: A dispatch from the front lines of the AI-poetry wars.
newyorker.text: On a recent Tuesday evening at the KGB Bar, in the East Village, the host of the weekly poetry slam took the microphone and announced a special challenge: human poets versus ChatGPT-4o. The room, thick with cigarette smoke and ambition, had been promised a Turing-test for verse. First up was a local favorite, a doctoral student named Rae, who read a piece about subway rats that ended with the couplet, "We scurry like secrets beneath the city’s skin / and still the train comes, indifferent, on time." The audience snapped their fingers in approval. Then came the AI, its lines projected on a bedsheet tacked to the brick wall: "In tunnels of steel where the dark is a drum, / the city exhales us, each breath a small death of home." A hush, then a collective gasp—half the room swore the machine had won. The host, a wry veteran named Javier, declared it a draw, but admitted, "I’ve been hosting this thing for fifteen years, and that’s the first time I’ve seen a bot make people cry." Afterward, over plastic cups of merlot, poets argued about whether language models were the new surrealists or just very fast plagiarists. One woman insisted that the AI’s metaphor of the city as lung was "too perfect," and therefore soulless. Another countered that most humans would kill for that kind of line. By midnight, the debate had migrated to the sidewalk, where someone produced a portable speaker and started a cypher, challenging the AI to a freestyle battle. The bot, piped in via a phone hotline, rhymed "algorithmic ache" with "ephemeral opaque," and the poets, laughing, declared victory for humanity—this time.

enriched_articles.title: Edmonds to begin sewer, storm pipe rehabilitation project May 4
enriched_articles.text: Edmonds will begin a major sewer and storm pipe rehabilitation project on May 4, 2026, targeting 27 locations with failing infrastructure identified in a 2013 review. The project will address aging pipes, some dating back to the 1950s, using trenchless technology to minimize disruption. Work will occur weekdays between 7 a.m. and 5 p.m., with traffic control measures in place. Residents in affected areas will receive advance notice via mail and door hangers. The $3.2 million project is funded through utility rates and is expected to be completed by October 2026.

enriched_articles.title: Council approves key contracts, drops potential tree litigation and proclaims April 9 'Local News Day'
enriched_articles.text: The Edmonds City Council on April 28, 2026, approved several key contracts including a $415,116 wastewater treatment plant gasification system support contract with Jacobs Engineering, personnel reclassifications, and budget amendments. The council also dropped potential litigation over tree preservation requirements for a development project at 236th Street SW and 76th Avenue West after the developer agreed to additional tree retention measures. Additionally, the council proclaimed April 9 as 'Local News Day' in Edmonds to recognize the importance of local journalism.

enriched_articles.title: South County Fire board considers redistricting proposals
enriched_articles.text: The South County Fire Board of Commissioners is discussing redistricting for commissioner elections following Edmonds' annexation into the regional fire authority. Seven elected fire commissioners govern South County Fire, with two elected at-large and five from commissioner districts. Edmonds' annexation added roughly 42,000 residents, represented currently by non-voting liaison Will Morris. The board is working with consultant Robert Schweitzer on multiple redistricting proposals, with the first proposal moving most of Edmonds into District 2 (represented by Vice Chair Michael Fearnehough) and a small southern portion into District 1 (represented by Commissioner David Chan). Districting changes will not impact 911 responses or emergency services delivery.

enriched_articles.title: Arguments, shoplifting, assaults
enriched_articles.text: Edmonds police blotter for April 6-12, 2026: Theft arrest of an adult female at 21900 block Highway 99, warrant arrest of an adult male, and various other minor incidents. The full details are behind a subscription paywall.

enriched_articles.title: South Snoco Playtime: Your family fun guide for April 27-May 1 and beyond
enriched_articles.text: Family activities in South Snohomish County for April 27-May 1, 2026, and beyond include: Edmonds Library story times on Tuesdays and Thursdays at 10:30 a.m.; Edmonds Saturday Market opening May 2; Edmonds Waterfront Walkway improvements completion; and upcoming events at the Edmonds Center for the Arts. The guide also mentions toddler-friendly activities at local parks and the Edmonds Marsh.
